narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid profile with an undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 38, which edges out the usual 36.70, along with coming from an upper-middle income family and planning full-time studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 72.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the school sees too much risk in your predicted bar performance, even with your strengths. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average fifth-decile position—which strongly signaled lower academic momentum heading into law school. Nearly as influential was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well under the typical fifth-decile mark, reinforcing concerns about early college struggles that often predict bar challenges. On a brighter note, your above-average undergraduate GPA pushed back against failure, helping to soften the prediction somewhat by showing later improvement. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your early low deciles stand out as unusually weak, while most successful applicants cluster around mid-deciles with steadier trajectories. Your LSAT and family background played smaller roles here, not enough to offset those early flags. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes these patterns for fairness across applicants. To move forward, focus on retaking or contextualizing those early grades, perhaps through a post-bac program or strong reference letters explaining your growth, then reapply to schools with holistic reviews. Building a narrative of resilience around your GPA rebound could also sway human reviewers next time. Keep pushing; your story has room to shine brighter.
